title: Results on abundance of global surfaces of section
speaker: Umberto Hryniewicz

proof_stages.label: Global surfaces of section as flow-to-map reductions
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines global and rational global surfaces of section for three-dimensional Reeb flows and motivates them through applications where the flow is reduced to a return map or a surface-bounded structure.

proof_stages.label: Right-handedness as a knot-theoretic abundance principle
proof_stages.summary: Knot-theoretic questions about periodic Reeb orbits lead to the notion of a right-handed flow, in which positive asymptotic linking of invariant measures forces finite collections of periodic orbits to form fibered links spanning global surfaces of section.

proof_stages.label: Rotation-linking criterion for right-handed Reeb flows
proof_stages.summary: For dynamically convex contact forms on the standard three-sphere, a parameter kappa measuring linearized rotation per linking with an unknotted binding is introduced, and the theorem kappa greater than two pi implies right-handedness is applied to pinched geodesic flows on two-spheres.

proof_stages.label: Generic rational global-section theorem
proof_stages.summary: The second main result is stated: C1-generic Reeb flows on closed three-manifolds, and C-infinity-generic contact forms on homology three-spheres, carry rational global surfaces of section.

proof_stages.label: Equidistribution and action-linking inputs
proof_stages.summary: Irie's equidistribution theorem and an action-linking lemma are combined to approximate the contact volume measure by weighted periodic orbits and thereby produce auxiliary periodic orbits linking positively with any prescribed finite collection.

proof_stages.label: Schwartzman-Sullivan asymptotic-cycle criterion
proof_stages.summary: A version of Schwartzman asymptotic cycle theory gives a criterion: if invariant measures in a link complement and the transverse rotations of the link components pair positively with a cohomology class, then a sublink spans a global surface of section.

proof_stages.label: Broken-book foliations and resolution by auxiliary orbits
proof_stages.summary: Holomorphic-curve broken-book foliations supply the geometric structure whose difficult positivity hypotheses can be checked after the auxiliary positive-linking orbits are added, so the asymptotic-cycle criterion yields the desired rational global surface of section.

examples.name: Birkhoff annulus for a positively curved two-sphere
examples.note: The annulus of unit tangent vectors along an embedded closed geodesic pointing into one chosen hemisphere makes the definition of a global surface of section concrete and later explains how curvature estimates enter the right-handedness application.
examples.engagement_reason: he constructs the Birkhoff annulus from unit vectors along an embedded geodesic and explains why positive curvature makes trajectories return to it

examples.name: Unknot, figure-eight knot, and non-fibered knots
examples.note: These knot examples illustrate the restriction imposed by right-handedness: fibered knots such as the unknot and figure-eight can occur, while non-fibered knots are excluded.

examples.name: Three-to-three broken-book foliation of the standard three-sphere
examples.note: The speaker works through this simplified broken-book picture to show why adding positively linking auxiliary orbits makes the Schwartzman positivity conditions hold, and then says the general proof repeats this mechanism with many binding orbits.
examples.engagement_reason: he draws and reasons through the three-to-three broken-book foliation, its orbits, leaves, blow-ups, cohomology classes, and positivity leading to a global section
